body.text: Henry Corbin opens with a confession: the word 'imaginary' will not do. It has been ruined by centuries of misuse, reduced to meaning unreal or fantasy. But the Persian and Arabic texts he translates describe something real, something you can visit, something with geography and inhabitants. He needs a new term.

In offering the two Latin words mundus imaginalis as the title of this discussion, I intend to treat a precise order of reality corresponding to a precise mode of perception, because Latin terminology gives the advantage of providing us with a technical and fixed point of reference, to which we can compare the various more-or-less irresolute equivalents that our modern Western languages suggest to us. I will make an immediate admission. The choice of these two words was imposed upon me some time ago, because it was impossible for me, in what I had to translate or say, to be satisfied with the word imaginary. Regardless of our efforts, we cannot prevent the term imaginary, in current usage that is not deliberate, from being equivalent to signifying unreal, something that is and remains outside of being and existence, something utopian.

Corbin spent years translating Sufi texts before he finally settled on 'imaginal' as the only adequate term. The word 'imaginary' would have betrayed everything those texts were trying to say. He needed a term that named something real rather than something made up.

So he coins 'imaginal' from the Latin 'imago' to name something that is neither imaginary nor intellectual, but a third thing: a real image that exists in its own order of reality. This is the mundus imaginalis. The Islamic theosophers he studies call it the 'eighth climate' — a region beyond the seven climate zones of the physical world. The term matters: where the seven climates are geographical, the eighth is ontological. It is not another place on the map but another mode of reality altogether.

If we usually speak of the imaginary as the unreal, the utopian, this must contain the symptom of something. In contrast to this something, we may examine the order of reality that I designate as mundus imaginalis, and what our theosophers in Islam designate as the 'eighth climate'; we will then examine the organ that perceives this reality, namely, the imaginative consciousness, the cognitive Imagination; and finally, we will present several examples that suggest to us the topography of these interworlds, as they have been seen by those who actually have been there.
The essay proceeds in three stages: first, the geography of the imaginal world; second, the faculty that perceives it; and third, examples from visionary traditions.

Corbin's first clue is a strange Persian term coined by the 12th-century philosopher Sohravardi: Na-koja-Abad, 'the land of No-where.' It sounds like utopia but means the opposite. Where utopia is an impossible fantasy, Na-koja-Abad is an actually existing place — just not in physical space.

Let us take the very beautiful tales, simultaneously visionary tales and tales of spiritual initiation, composed in Persian by Sohravardi. Each time, the visionary finds himself torn away from the geographical earth that he knows, and he enters into a relationship with a mysterious being who appears to him, a being who is the Perfect Nature, the active Intelligence, the Guardian Angel of humanity. The itinerary of this visionary geography reveals a region that does not appear on any map of the physical world, yet is not a fiction. Na-koja-Abad is a strange term. It does not occur in any Persian dictionary. It was coined by Sohravardi himself. Literally, it signifies the city, the country or land of No-where. It may appear as the exact equivalent of the term utopia, and yet with regard to the concept, the intention, and the true meaning, I believe that we would be guilty of mistranslation.

Why is it not utopia? Because utopia is unreal. Na-koja-Abad has extension — the Persian word 'abad' means a cultivated land, a city. It has topography, cities, inhabitants, and relations between them. It is simply not located in sensory space. The question 'where is it' gets a real answer: it begins on the convex surface of the Ninth Sphere, the limit of the cosmos, and from there opens into a geography that mirrors the soul's interior.

The word Na-koja-Abad does not designate something like unextended being. The Persian word 'abad' signifies a city, a cultivated land, thus something extended. The extension of that land is not of the same order as the extension of a physical city. But it is an extension. Sohravardi represents the composite of the mystical cities: Jabalqa, Jabarsa, and Hurqalya. Topographically, this region begins on the convex surface of the Ninth Sphere, the Sphere that includes the whole of the cosmos, the limit of physical space. It is a matter of entering, passing into the interior and, paradoxically, finding oneself outside. Henceforth, it is spiritual reality that envelops, surrounds, contains the reality called material. That is why spiritual reality is not in the where. It is the where that is in it.

This reversal — that spirit contains matter, not the reverse — is what the 'eighth climate' names. In medieval geography, the world was divided into seven climate zones. The eighth climate is not an extra zone on that map. It is a different kind of space entirely: the space opened by the reversal itself. To enter it is to pass beyond the mountain of Qaf, the cosmic mountain that rings the physical world. On the other side, distance is no longer measured in miles but in spiritual affinity. Two souls side by side in physical space may be worlds apart here; two souls separated by oceans may be neighbors.

Let us pause a moment before the topography that Sohravardi describes in the 'Rustling of Gabriel's Wings.' The traveler meets a being, his Perfect Nature, his Angel. This being tells him: 'If you wish to know the Cities, know that our city, the city of the Angel, lies beyond the mountain of Qaf.' The mountain of Qaf is the cosmic mountain that surrounds the physical world. To pass beyond it is to enter the eighth climate.

Having placed the imaginal world beyond the mountain of Qaf, Corbin now asks what organ could possibly see it. If this realm is not physical, the physical senses cannot perceive it. If it is not purely intellectual, the intellect alone cannot grasp it. A third faculty is needed, and its name is the active Imagination.

Our authors distinguish between three universes. First, the sensory world, the world of physical experience. Second, the world of pure Intelligences, the realm of archetypes and angels. And between these two is placed an intermediate world, which our authors designate as 'alam al-mithal, the world of the Image, mundus imaginalis: a world as ontologically real as the world of the senses and the world of the intellect, a world that requires a faculty of perception belonging to it. That faculty is the imaginative power, the one we must avoid confusing with the imagination that modern man identifies with fantasy and that produces only the imaginary. The active Imagination is the preeminent mirror, the epiphanic place of the Images of the archetypal world.

This is the most important idea in the essay. In modern thought, imagination is subjective: it projects, it fantasizes, it makes things up. For Corbin, the active Imagination is receptive. It is a mirror. It does not create the archetypal Images, it receives them and gives them form. This is what makes it an organ of knowledge rather than of escape. The same substantial reality takes on different forms at each level: a pure Intelligence in the intelligible world becomes an Image in the imaginal world and a physical object in the sensory world. The Imagination is the faculty that traces this continuity across levels.

It is a function that leads us to represent that the same substantial realities assume forms corresponding to each universe. What is a pure Intelligence in the intelligible world becomes an Image in the imaginal world and a physical object in the sensory world. The active Imagination permits rigorous analogical knowledge. It escapes the dualism of matter and spirit, the divorce that has haunted Western philosophy since Descartes. Through the Imagination, we can know the subtle body, the intermediate reality that links the soul to the body. That world is the world of subtle bodies, the idea of which proves indispensable if one wishes to describe a link between the pure spirit and the material body.

Corbin brings in Swedenborg as a Western witness to the same principle. The 18th-century Swedish visionary described heaven in terms that exactly match the logic of Na-koja-Abad: space in heaven corresponds to states of interior, and change of place is change of state.

Swedenborg explains that all changes of place in the spiritual world are effected by changes of state in the interiors. Spaces in heaven are simply the external conditions corresponding to the internal states. Such a description is eminently appropriate to Na-koja-Abad and its mysterious Cities. In relation to the corporeal place, the spiritual place is a No-where.

The distance between two souls in the spiritual world is measured by difference in inner state, not miles. This is the principle that makes visionary geography consistent across traditions: the same inner states produce the same landscapes. Not because everyone is hallucinating the same thing, but because the imaginal world has stable structures that appear to anyone who reaches the appropriate state.

Corbin closes with a challenge: any complete cosmology must account for this intermediate world. The subtle body, the imaginal realm, the active Imagination — these are not luxuries for mystics. They are the missing terms that connect matter to spirit, body to soul, physics to metaphysics. Without them, we are trapped in a flat universe where everything is either measurable matter or subjective fantasy.

We must, at all events, have available a cosmology of such a kind that the most astounding information of modern science remains inferior to it. This cosmology must account for the subtle body, the idea of which proves indispensable if one wishes to describe a link between the pure spirit and the material body.

Such is the constitution of the intermediate world, the mundus imaginalis, the world that is the place of the soul and the place of the subtle body. It is the world where everything that is separated finds its union, where everything that is dispersed finds its gathering. It is the world of the soul's true country, the homeland to which the mystic returns when he has completed the journey of the outer life.
